title: Matrix-noise Jacobians in stochastic-calculus inference and optimal paths
abstract: Multiplicative noise makes stochastic dynamics depend on how the white-noise limit is interpreted. In multidimensional systems with matrix-valued noise amplitudes $σ(x)$, this dependence includes a local Jacobian contribution that is absent from the scalar examples most often used to build intuition. We formulate a finite-step path-likelihood framework for $θ$-discretized diffusions and show that its short-time expansion isolates the scalar $J_σ=\partial_jσ_{ik}\partial_iσ_{jk}-(\partial_iσ_{ik})(\partial_lσ_{lk})$. For a specified noise-amplitude representation $σ$, this quantity vanishes in one-dimensional, scalar-isotropic, and strictly diagonal cases, but can survive when state-dependent noise directions mix different components. We then test its consequences using paired comparisons that hold the drift, diffusion matrix, interpolation point, and Gaussian increment term fixed. In Model A, removing only the off-diagonal determinant contribution produces a shift in the fitted stochastic prescription that vanishes when $J_σ=0$. In Model B, removing the corresponding state-dependent action term changes a stable optimized transition path. These results show that a genuinely matrix-noise part of the short-time path measure can survive the scalar cancellations familiar from simpler settings and produce measurable changes in fitted stochastic prescriptions and Onsager--Machlup paths.
summary: The paper reveals that in multidimensional systems with matrix-valued noise, a unique Jacobian term arises that is ignored in simpler scalar models. This term can significantly bias the estimation of stochastic calculus conventions and physically alter the predicted most-likely paths in a system.
main_problem: Investigating how matrix-valued multiplicative noise affects stochastic-calculus inference and the calculation of optimal paths in multidimensional systems.
main_result: The paper identifies a specific matrix-noise Jacobian term, J_sigma, that is absent in scalar-noise models and demonstrates that it causes measurable shifts in inferred stochastic prescriptions and deforms optimized transition paths.
method: The author uses a finite-step path-likelihood framework, short-time expansions, and a variational Hamiltonian approach to derive and test the impact of the matrix-Jacobian term.
model_or_system: The study uses multidimensional stochastic differential equations with multiplicative noise, specifically testing two models: Model A (a 2D system for inference validation) and Model B (a variational setting for path deformation).
key_observables: The matrix-noise Jacobian (J_sigma), the paired estimator shift (theta_diag - theta_full), and the deformation of the Onsager-Machlup optimal path.
important_parameters: The stochastic discretization parameter (theta), the noise-amplitude coefficients (b and c), and the time-step (delta t).
main_assumptions: Assumes smooth coefficients, non-singular noise amplitude matrices, and the small time-step limit (delta t -> 0).
figures_summary: Figure 1 (not fully described) and Figure 3 show the most likely transition paths for Model B, visually demonstrating a 'bowing' effect caused by the matrix-Jacobian term.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of matrix-noise ambiguity, formulates a path-likelihood framework, identifies the J_sigma term via short-time expansion, and then validates the findings through numerical inference tests (Model A) and variational path optimization (Model B).
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for open quantum systems and many-body dynamics, as it shows that neglecting off-diagonal noise correlations can lead to incorrect physical conclusions about the underlying stochastic dynamics and transition paths.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Representative likelihood curves for Model A. The full determinant likelihood and the reduced-determinant con- trol are evaluated on the same trajectory. The omitted off- diagonal determinant contribution shifts the maximum of the likelihood in θ.

FIG. 2. Robustness of the paired inference shift in Model A under changes of the generating prescription. The plotted quantity is the paired estimator shift ˆθdiag −ˆθfull for θ0 = 0.25, 0.50, 0.75. The shift vanishes at bc = 0 and remains visible for nonzero bc, showing that the effect is not tied to the Stratonovich value θ0 = 1/2. Error bars denote SEM over independent seeds.

FIG. 3. Most likely transition paths for Model B. The full action contains the state-dependent matrix-Jacobian term Jσ = 4bcx, while the reduced-action control omits it. The full optimal path bows into negative x, as predicted by Eq. (12).
